channel.title: NPR Topics: Science
channel.description: The latest health and science news. Updates on medicine, healthy living, nutrition, drugs, diet, and advances in science and technology. Subscribe to the Health & Science podcast.
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: How to map quadrillions of miles of underground fungi
item.description: NPR's Adrian Florido speaks with ecologist Justin Stewart about mapping the complex network of fungi connecting the Earth's plants.
item.content:encoded: NPR's Adrian Florido speaks with ecologist Justin Stewart about mapping the complex network of fungi connecting the Earth's plants.
item.dc:creator: Zephyr Weinreich

item.title: In Albania, anger grows against the government for supporting a Kushner-linked luxury resort
item.description: Albania's government has given preliminary approval to plans for the luxury resort along a stretch of coastline, prompting daily protests and legal challenges by environmental groups.
item.content:encoded:
Albania's government has given preliminary approval to plans for the luxury resort along a stretch of coastline, prompting daily protests and legal challenges by environmental groups.

(Image credit: Vlasov Sulaj)
item.dc:creator: Rob Schmitz

item.title: Is a transparent fish the future of brain science? This center is betting on it
item.description: One of the world's leading brain research centers is shifting away from fruit flies and toward a tiny, transparent fish. The goal: to understand how brains control the behavior of an animal or human.
item.content:encoded:
One of the world's leading brain research centers is shifting away from fruit flies and toward a tiny, transparent fish. The goal: to understand how brains control the behavior of an animal or human.

(Image credit: Chie Satou)
item.dc:creator: Jon Hamilton

item.title: Why a brain research lab is switching from fruit flies to tiny fish
item.description: One of the world's leading brain research labs is switching from fruit flies to a tiny, transparent fish. The goal is to observe an entire animal's brain at work.
item.content:encoded: One of the world's leading brain research labs is switching from fruit flies to a tiny, transparent fish. The goal is to observe an entire animal's brain at work.
item.dc:creator: Jon Hamilton

item.title: In Oregon, a newly discovered species of spider got its own name and naming ceremony
item.description: A biologist recently discovered a new species of cave spider in Oregon's Columbia River Gorge. It's getting a name from the Confederated Tribes and Bands of the Yakama Nation.
item.content:encoded: A biologist recently discovered a new species of cave spider in Oregon's Columbia River Gorge. It's getting a name from the Confederated Tribes and Bands of the Yakama Nation.
item.dc:creator: Deena Prichep

item.title: COMIC: How excessive heat kills and how to stay safe
item.description: Human bodies have a natural cooling system, but it can do only so much in high temperatures and humidity. Here's the science behind how heat kills. And how to protect yourself.
item.content:encoded: Human bodies have a natural cooling system, but it can do only so much in high temperatures and humidity. Here's the science behind how heat kills. And how to protect yourself.
item.dc:creator: Maria Godoy

item.title: New study finds 'foundation' species continue to shape ecosystems after their death
item.description: Foundation species like coral, oysters and big trees are critical to their ecosystems, providing food and shelter. A new study finds their influence continues after their death.
item.content:encoded: Foundation species like coral, oysters and big trees are critical to their ecosystems, providing food and shelter. A new study finds their influence continues after their death.
item.dc:creator: Nathan Rott

item.title: This week's Short Wave news roundup
item.description: NPR's Short Wave team talks about how air pollution affects the brain, what ancient squirrel poop reveals about prehistoric fauna, and a whale graveyard on the ocean floor.
item.content:encoded: NPR's Short Wave team talks about how air pollution affects the brain, what ancient squirrel poop reveals about prehistoric fauna, and a whale graveyard on the ocean floor.
item.dc:creator: Angela Zhang

item.title: Socioeconomic factors are becoming 'biologically embedded' in children's brains
item.description: A study of more than 2,300 9- to 10-year-olds found that socioeconomic factors explained most differences in the preteens' brain development.
item.content:encoded:
A study of more than 2,300 9- to 10-year-olds found that socioeconomic factors explained most differences in the preteens' brain development.

(Image credit: Andriy Onufriyenko)
item.dc:creator: Jon Hamilton

item.title: From neon mosquitoes to winged migrations, top images captured by scientists
item.description: Five winning images from a photo contest show scientists at work and capture the wonder of research and discovery.
item.content:encoded:
Five winning images from a photo contest show scientists at work and capture the wonder of research and discovery.

(Image credit: Shayanta Chowdhury)
item.dc:creator: Ari Daniel
